content: The most common mistake beginners make when migrating from standard library error handling to crates like color-eyre is the assumption that adding a panic handler will magically fix all output issues, whereas in reality, the real problem usually lies in how you define your error types and capture context. You likely know what an Option<T> or Result<T, E> looks like since they appear everywhere in Rust standard library code, but when you try to implement those same patterns with color-eyre, the default behavior of a simple enum often results in plain text output that lacks the rich terminal formatting you expect from this crate. The core misunderstanding stems from believing that error messages are static strings rather than dynamic structures composed of named fields and optional span traces. When you define an error type like Error { code: u16, message: String } without using the Section trait or SpanTrace macro, your application will output raw JSON or verbose struct representations instead of the colored, formatted messages color-eyre provides by design. The solution requires shifting your mental model from treating errors as simple strings to viewing them as structured data where each named field becomes a distinct section in the terminal output when rendered via the Section trait. When you implement this correctly using error macros like `error!` and `span_trace!`, color-eyre automatically generates the necessary formatting logic for you, but if you manually construct an Error type without leveraging these abstractions, you will see garbled output because the crate cannot identify which fields should become sections versus plain text. Furthermore, many developers forget that while panic handlers can print custom messages using `std::panic::catch_unwind`, they do not automatically include span traces or section headers unless those values are explicitly attached to the error object itself before it bubbles up to a handler. The critical insight is that color-eyre relies on the Section trait and the SpanTrace implementation to map your error fields to terminal sections, so if you define an error type without implementing these traits for your custom fields, the panic handler will simply print the raw data structure without any formatting. To avoid this pitfall entirely, you should define all your error types using the `error!` macro which ensures every field is recognized as a potential section, and when capturing context like thread ID or location via `span_trace!`, ensure these values are included in the error variant itself rather than trying to inject them later in a panic handler. This approach guarantees that even if you restart your application with different environment variables or run on different systems, your terminal output remains consistent because the formatting logic is baked into the error definition itself. You might wonder why not just use `std::fmt` directly for all errors; the answer is that color-eyre provides a specialized rendering engine specifically optimized for Rust's type system to automatically detect section boundaries and apply ANSI escape codes only where needed, which eliminates the boilerplate of manually formatting strings with manual string interpolation. By following this pattern—defining errors with named fields, using `error!` macros, attaching spans via `span_trace!`, and letting color-eyre handle the Section trait implementation—you achieve professional-grade terminal output that clearly distinguishes error categories, provides actionable context through traces, and maintains readability even in complex applications. This method not only solves the immediate formatting problem but also establishes a robust foundation for future debugging by ensuring every error carries its own contextual metadata rather than relying on external log messages or panic handlers alone.

The most common and destructive mistake beginners make when adopting the `color-eyre` crate is treating error formatting as a mere cosmetic layer rather than a fundamental part of their error propagation strategy. They install the library, write a simple panic handler that catches errors from standard libraries like `std::io`, and expect to see pretty-printed output in production logs without understanding how the underlying data structures actually carry context across boundaries. This leads to silent failures where rich terminal output is never rendered because the code path simply never reaches the point where the formatted error string is constructed or logged, leaving developers with cryptic stack traces instead of actionable diagnostics. The root cause lies in a misunderstanding of how `SpanTrace` interacts with the Rust type system and panic propagation mechanisms. When you use `eyre::Result`, the error object is not just a simple value; it is a structured aggregate that carries span information embedded within its implementation details, specifically through the `span_trace()` function which attaches contextual metadata to the error instance itself. This metadata travels with the error through any chain of `unwrap` or `expect` calls because these operations propagate the entire result object rather than just extracting its value. However, if your panic handler is written incorrectly—for example, by catching a generic panic but ignoring the original cause or re-packing it into a new simple string without preserving the span trace—you lose the rich context entirely. The correct approach demands that you configure a global panic hook using `std::panic::set_hook` which intercepts panics before they terminate the program and allows you to inspect the actual panic payload, ensuring that when an error is caught in your custom handler or when it bubbles up via `eyre::Result`, the associated span data remains intact within the error structure. You must explicitly invoke the `section()` method on your error builder to add extra context like file paths, user IDs, or internal state before the error is finalized; this section becomes part of the span trace and appears in the terminal output as a clearly delimited block beneath the primary error message. Without calling `section()`, you are left with barebones diagnostics that fail to meet the needs of complex systems where context is as important as the error itself. Furthermore, beginners often forget that `color-eyre` relies on the `std::panic` mechanism and does not automatically re-export panic handling capabilities; they must manually install a hook that formats errors using `eyre::Error::pretty()` or similar methods to generate the rich terminal output you desire. If your application panics in an environment without a configured handler, such as inside a thread spawned by `std::thread::spawn` or within a Tokio runtime task, the pretty-printed error will never appear because those threads do not execute through the same panic hook setup. This forces you to either ensure all threads share the global panic hook configuration or manually implement panic recovery logic within each critical thread, which is often unnecessary if you understand that `eyre` errors are designed to be propagated cleanly through async and sync contexts alike once the basic infrastructure is in place. The depth of this topic requires recognizing that error handling is not just about catching exceptions but about designing a system where context travels as naturally as data, and failing to do so results in brittle applications that cannot effectively communicate failures to users or operators. By grounding your design in the principle that every piece of diagnostic information must be attached early via `section()` and preserved through every layer using the native Rust panic mechanism with `color-eyre`, you ensure that your system provides clear, actionable feedback even when things go wrong.
